topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt too bright, reflecting off the glossy surface of the piece I’d spent six months perfecting. Then I saw it—a subtle, almost hidden line of ink tracing the edge of my most intricate shadow work. My breath hitched. It was a design, unmistakably mine, yet executed with a precision that felt alien, a deliberate mirroring of my own hand. A tight knot formed in my chest, a sudden, sharp clenching in my jaw. I walked over, my steps suddenly heavy, and leaned in close enough to see the faint, almost invisible shading. "Who did this?" My voice came out thin, brittle. The artist, a young woman with startlingly calm eyes, looked up, a flicker of surprise crossing her face before she offered a slow, unreadable smile. The air between us thickened, charged with unspoken comparison.

[story 2]

I stared at the framed canvas, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the pedestal. Every stroke, every deliberate curve, was a testament to my unique vision. But then, tucked beneath the vibrant blues of my latest seascape, there was a pattern—a miniature rendition of a constellation only I had ever sketched. It was undeniably *my* work, yet it bore the signature of another. A slow burn started low in my stomach, a restless churning that made my hands tremble slightly. I paced the perimeter of the room, my gaze darting everywhere but the canvas. When the creator approached, radiating an easy confidence, I kept my tone deliberately cool, almost dismissive. "That detail," I managed, my voice pitched just a shade too high, "it’s quite familiar. Did you borrow from somewhere?" I watched her reaction closely, searching for any sign that she understood the sudden shift in my composure.

[story 3]

The unveiling was supposed to be a moment of pure triumph, a validation of all the sleepless nights. Instead, my focus narrowed to a tiny, almost imperceptible mark on the lower quadrant of the portrait. It was a complex filigree, a motif I’d been agonizing over for weeks. Someone had taken my soul, rendered it in permanent pigment. A flush crept up my neck, warm and undeniable, spreading across my cheeks. I felt a strange, hot pressure behind my eyes, a sudden urge to dismantle everything around me. I approached the figure hesitantly, my usual assuredness entirely absent. "This," I stated, my voice low and edged with something sharp, "this particular flourish... it reminds me of a sketch I made months ago. A very specific one." I waited, my posture rigid, daring her to contradict the strange resonance in my tone.
